SPEAKER_00: "The great exhibition of 1851 in London showcased the new wallpaper designs and styles."
SPEAKER_00: "There you had thousands of exhibits that were meant to celebrate how good contemporary manufacturing was."

SPEAKER_00: "There were a lot of very detailed patterns and bright colors."
SPEAKER_00: "Some papers had very realistic pictures and others had special effects like the illusion of depth."
SPEAKER_00: "So you'd think this would be good."

SPEAKER_00: "The British Inspector General for Art actually wrote a report specifically about the wallpapers at the exposition."
SPEAKER_00: "In his view, the judges gave awards based on technical aspects, like the number of colors used in the papers, rather than on more traditional aesthetic criteria, like beauty or simplicity."
SPEAKER_00: "Despite being a leader in industrial progress, England, by some accounts, seemed to be lagging behind its rival France in terms of design quality."
SPEAKER_00: "And some design reformers worried that the nation could lose its advantage in international trade if designs weren't changed."
SPEAKER_00: "So the new technical abilities in production introduced by the Industrial Revolution prompted or enabled these new wallpaper designs."
SPEAKER_00: "You see, manufacturers had figured out how to use engraved metal rollers in the production of wallpaper."
SPEAKER_00: "Using metal rollers to print wallpaper allowed a level of extremely fine detail and shading that previously had not been possible."
SPEAKER_00: "I mean, before, the papers were being made by block printing, which was pretty imprecise."
SPEAKER_00: "Plus, the new production techniques made wallpaper a lot cheaper to buy, since it reduced production costs."
SPEAKER_00: "And these new wallpapers were popular."
SPEAKER_00: "To the average industrialist, good design meant whatever sold the best."
SPEAKER_00: "Popular taste equated ornate embellishment with excellence, so many manufacturers created wallpapers with as much surface decoration as possible."
SPEAKER_00: "Graphic elements from different eras were combined without regard to period or stylistic unity, and designs became increasingly cluttered, all of which irritated design purists."
SPEAKER_00: "Even the Victorian-era writers mocked the wallpaper design trend."
SPEAKER_00: "In his 1852 short story, A House Full of Horrors, the writer Henry Morley described the garish combination of mismatched design elements that represented the new style in wallpaper design."
SPEAKER_00: "Quote, the paper in my parlor contains four kinds of birds of paradise besides bridges and pagodas."
SPEAKER_00: "Wilkie Collins described another of the new wallpapers as, quote, a gaudy pattern of birds, trellis work, and flowers in gold, red, and green on a white background, end quote."
SPEAKER_00: "One technique in particular became associated with the new wallpapers, Trump-Loy."
SPEAKER_00: "Trump-Loy, that's French for tricking or cheating the eye, was a design technique that created visual illusions."
SPEAKER_00: "So you'd have wallpaper that actually looked like marble or wood grain or maybe like aged leather."
SPEAKER_00: "Now, the design reform critics, they opposed this."
SPEAKER_00: "They thought that Trump-Loy designs were inherently deceptive because they made walls look like something they were not."
SPEAKER_00: "This was also, this was the design reformers critique of the three-dimensional illusions of depth."
SPEAKER_00: "To them, it was all a type of lying."
SPEAKER_00: "So these designs were actually immoral."
SPEAKER_00: "And according to their shaky logic, if people filled their houses with wallpapers that were visually deceitful, then the inhabitants would become deceitful too."
SPEAKER_00: "Yeah, right."
SPEAKER_00: "When they created wallpapers, design reform advocates avoided illusions and visual deceptions."
SPEAKER_00: "They created primarily geometric two-dimensional patterns, which they said would confirm the wall as a flat surface rather than disguise or misrepresent it."

SPEAKER_00: "As it goes underground it heats up because in these fields there are magma chambers only a few kilometers below the ocean floor."

SPEAKER_00: "So, you're less likely to be eaten, more likely to survive if you happen to be bigger."

SPEAKER_00: "They need less food per pound to keep themselves going."

SPEAKER_00: "He was very much set against theories suggesting that evolution tends to drive in a certain direction, tends to direct towards certain ends."

SPEAKER_00: "Cope found what he needed in order to support his theory, but he didn't really prove it."

SPEAKER_00: "He claimed, and there's a certain amount of foundation to this, that when a natural disaster wipes out large numbers of individuals in a species, they often tend to wipe out the bigger individuals more quickly."
SPEAKER_00: "Well, let's say it gets really cold, and your food supply is cut off."

SPEAKER_00: "So what Gould suggested is that after a natural disaster, evolution, which is random, might not look random."

SPEAKER_00: "Animals were going to have to get bigger after a disaster, no matter what."

SPEAKER_00: "It's not evolution directing towards larger sizes."

SPEAKER_00: "And the prevailing theory back then was the core accretion theory."
SPEAKER_00: "According to the core accretion theory, when the sun formed in the middle of a huge, flat, spinning disk of gas and dust, there were some leftovers, stuff that didn't go into making the sun."
SPEAKER_00: "And as the disk kept swirling around, bits of matter collided, and some stuck together in clumps."
SPEAKER_00: "And over millions of years, some clumps gathered, accreted, more and more material, and eventually grew large enough to form rocky cores."
SPEAKER_00: "But due to the sun's energy over long periods of time, the cores of planets closer to it couldn't hold on to light gases very well."
SPEAKER_00: "So they remained small, rocky planets like Earth."
SPEAKER_00: "Meanwhile, though, the planets farther out from the Sun, their gravitational force kept on pulling in gas molecules."
SPEAKER_00: "And these colder planets could accrete and hold onto huge amounts of gas."
SPEAKER_00: "And you ended up with giant planets, such as Jupiter, composed largely of gas."
SPEAKER_00: "So the final result of this accretion is what you see in our solar system, rocky planets nearer the sun and gas giants farther out."
SPEAKER_00: "And then there's the oddball Pluto which we'll just ignore for the time being."
SPEAKER_00: "Well, in 1995, a new planet was discovered."
SPEAKER_00: "In orbit, not around our sun, but around a different sun, a star called 51 Pegasi."
SPEAKER_00: "And this was not just a great first in itself, but the discovery of 51 Pegasi B, that's what they named this first extrasolar planet,"
SPEAKER_00: "This immediately challenged the core accretion theory of planet formation."
SPEAKER_00: "That's because 51 Pegasi B is a gas giant that orbits very close to its sun."
SPEAKER_00: "In fact, eight times closer to its sun than Mercury, which is the closest planet to our sun."
SPEAKER_00: "Eight times closer."
SPEAKER_00: "And a gas giant, which theory would not have predicted so near."
SPEAKER_00: "Clearly, a different theory of planet formation was needed."
SPEAKER_00: "hence the disc instability theory."
SPEAKER_00: "The disc instability theory had been around for a while, but hadn't gained much attention until the discovery of 51 Pegasi B."
SPEAKER_00: "The theory basically says that if the gaseous disc around the newborn star is massive enough and cold enough"
SPEAKER_00: "then any unevenness of gravity within the disk could cause clumps, even giant clumps of gases to form in as little as a thousand years."
SPEAKER_00: "And those clumps could then become gas giant planets."
SPEAKER_00: "So the disk instability theory says giant planets could form near a sun without needing a long time to do it."
SPEAKER_00: "Funny, isn't it?"
SPEAKER_00: "How one little, okay, one momentous discovery can change what we thought we knew was true."
SPEAKER_00: "Well, as if it wasn't enough to have these two competing theories of planet formation, another one somewhat related to the core accretion theory was then proposed."
SPEAKER_00: "Some astronomers remain unconvinced that a gas giant planet could form so close to a sun."
SPEAKER_00: "Among other things, they doubt that gases would last very long there because the sun's intense radiation could just blow them away."
SPEAKER_00: "And so they support the idea of orbital migration."
SPEAKER_00: "The orbital migration theory suggests that a gas giant planet forms far from its sun, but then over time moves to a position even closer to that sun than where you'd expect to see smaller rocky planets."
SPEAKER_00: "And the powerful gravity of such a large planet holds on to its massive atmosphere, even when it's so close to that sun."
SPEAKER_00: "So we find this one gas giant planet orbiting very close to its sun, and then, well, it turns out that 51 Pegasi B is not at all unique."
SPEAKER_00: "that it's just the first of a number of large, gaseous planets we found that we like to call hot Jupiters because they're about the size of Jupiter, or larger, and close enough to their suns to be very, very hot."

SPEAKER_02: "But it's not just microbes from other planets traveling to Earth that scientists are studying."
SPEAKER_02: "Going in the other direction, they're looking at whether microbes from Earth might have been carried to other planets."

SPEAKER_02: "But first, well, we better back up a bit."

SPEAKER_02: "When these objects hit a planet's surface, they form a crater, a hole, and material, well, rocks, get ejected from that crater and thrown up into the planet's atmosphere."
SPEAKER_02: "Some of the rocks might even escape the planet's gravity and go into outer space."
SPEAKER_02: "Okay, most of the life in the ejected rocks would die due to the extremely high pressures and the high speed of the rocks as they're being thrown into space."

SPEAKER_02: "Uh, yes?"
SPEAKER_00: "I don't see how that could happen."
SPEAKER_00: "I thought anything living would die in outer space."
SPEAKER_02: "There's no air, no food."
SPEAKER_02: "Yes, and there's extreme temperatures."
SPEAKER_02: "Cosmic rays, ultraviolet radiation."
SPEAKER_02: "But experiments involving bacteria in space have shown that they can survive these conditions."
SPEAKER_02: "If a microbe is deep enough inside a rock, it'll be protected from ultraviolet light, which is particularly deadly."
SPEAKER_02: "Also, if a microbe forms a spore, it has an even better chance of survival."
SPEAKER_02: "I should explain that a spore is a dormant structure formed by some organisms for reproduction or to help in survival."
SPEAKER_02: "They have a protective coating to help resist harsh environments, and they have only one or a few cells."
SPEAKER_02: "Cold temperatures actually seem to help spores survive, so a microbe that has developed a spore might even survive in rocks for millions of years."
SPEAKER_02: "Okay, so imagine rocks being thrown up into space because of an impact on a planet in our own solar system."

SPEAKER_02: "The people in charge of the Galileo mission didn't want the probe to crash into Europa and contaminate it with microbes from Earth that it might have been carrying."

prompt_text:
Create a visual aid for the passage:

SPEAKER_00: "So we've spent a lot of time in this class studying ecosystems."
SPEAKER_00: "And now I want to talk about a specific instance of an ecosystem changing over time, changing significantly and unexpectedly."
SPEAKER_00: "I'm talking about the Aleutian Islands off the coast of Alaska."
SPEAKER_00: "The Aleutian Islands up until the 18th century were a grassland ecosystem."
SPEAKER_00: "Now the grasslands on some of the islands have disappeared and what we have there now is tundra, primarily low scrubby vegetation."
SPEAKER_00: "And this started happening in the 18th century."
SPEAKER_00: "Okay, so why?"
SPEAKER_00: "Jeff?"
SPEAKER_01: "Um, excuse me Professor, but I thought tundra referred to frozen ground."
SPEAKER_01: "Wouldn't all the islands have that?"
SPEAKER_00: "Okay, right."
SPEAKER_00: "Tundra often refers to a region where the underground soil is permanently frozen."
SPEAKER_00: "And the primary vegetation is moss, lichen and maybe a few dwarf shrubs."
SPEAKER_00: "But it's the vegetation that classifies a region as tundra, not the temperature of the soil."
SPEAKER_00: "Anyway, in the 18th century, traders introduced foxes onto the islands and those foxes preyed on the ground nesting seabirds."
SPEAKER_01: "And you're saying that changed the entire ecosystem?"
SPEAKER_00: "Oh yes, and drastically."
SPEAKER_00: "Introducing a single species can gradually transform an entire ecosystem."
SPEAKER_00: "And a study that was done on the Illusion Island shows one way this can happen."
SPEAKER_00: "So, alright."
SPEAKER_00: "The foxes were introduced, and the most obvious impact was a decrease of the seabird population."
SPEAKER_00: "That's a direct impact, right?"
SPEAKER_00: "The foxes preyed on the seabirds and they started to disappear."
SPEAKER_00: "Well, there were indirect effects too."
SPEAKER_00: "Changes in the soil and vegetation."
SPEAKER_00: "And these effects weren't obvious for a long time."
SPEAKER_00: "They were much harder to detect."
SPEAKER_00: "So, this study."
SPEAKER_00: "A biologist led a team of researchers to the Aleutian Islands, where they noticed significant differences in the islands that had foxes and the islands that didn't, especially in the vegetation."
SPEAKER_00: "It was so thick on the islands without foxes that they could barely get through it."
SPEAKER_00: "But on the islands with foxes, it was only up to their ankles."
SPEAKER_00: "And the biologist had a theory about this."
SPEAKER_00: "Can anyone guess what it might be?"
SPEAKER_01: "Hmm."
SPEAKER_01: "So the foxes are eating the seabirds."
SPEAKER_01: "Maybe the, um, the seabirds were using up the grass, like for nesting materials or something."
SPEAKER_00: "Well, think about what that would mean."
SPEAKER_01: "Oh, right."
SPEAKER_00: "What the biologist thought was, he thought it might have something to do with the soil nutrients."
SPEAKER_00: "So these researchers, to investigate this theory, they analyzed the soil on these islands."
SPEAKER_00: "They wanted to determine the nutrient content to see if it played some kind of role in transforming the vegetation from grassland to tundra."
SPEAKER_00: "Well, what they found was, I think, very interesting."
SPEAKER_00: "Now first, the soil on the Aleutian Islands is volcanic soil, nutrient-poor soil that formed from volcanic rock."
SPEAKER_00: "And what the researchers learned was that the islands without foxes were receiving regular doses of fertilizer from the droppings of the seabirds the foxes preyed on."
SPEAKER_00: "These seabirds eat fish, and they were bringing ocean-derived nutrients to the islands in the form of droppings when they came there to nest."
SPEAKER_00: "Now the islands with foxes weren't getting this fertilizer because the birds were disappearing."
SPEAKER_00: "So now we see the indirect effects of the foxes, right?"
SPEAKER_00: "The foxes, because of their impact on the seabirds, deprived the soil of fertilizer which caused the grasslands to disappear and be replaced by tundra."
SPEAKER_00: "Tundra vegetation does well in nutrient-poor soil."
SPEAKER_00: "Now that's a pretty huge impact and one that no one predicted."
SPEAKER_00: "So what can we do about it?"
SPEAKER_00: "Well, we want to bring back the seabirds and we're doing that."
SPEAKER_00: "We're removing the foxes and we've been successful."
SPEAKER_00: "Most of the foxes are off the islands now and the seabirds are starting to come back."
SPEAKER_00: "But seabirds, most seabirds, that is, have only one offspring a year."
SPEAKER_00: "Well, we've learned that there are now only half the number of breeding seabirds on the islands that had the foxes as there are on the islands that didn't."
SPEAKER_00: "And there were major changes in the vegetation, and the foxes were there for a long time."
SPEAKER_00: "So, does this mean that non-native species should never be introduced into an ecosystem?"
SPEAKER_00: "Well, not necessarily."
SPEAKER_00: "Scientists introduce species in many different places around the world and for a number of reasons."
SPEAKER_00: "We'll talk more about that over the course of the semester."
SPEAKER_00: "It does point out the dangers of unforeseen consequences, though, and it certainly suggests the need for careful planning and for further studies of the effects in habitats such as these where new species have already been introduced."

prompt_text:
Create a visual aid for the passage:

SPEAKER_00: "Yesterday during our discussion of realism in art, I made reference to some new art forms and we'll be talking about several of these over the next few days, prints, watercolors, landscapes."
SPEAKER_00: "But I want to start with one you're probably less familiar with, panoramas."
SPEAKER_00: "Panoramas were huge canvases, sometimes several stories tall, and they were intended to be viewed in a circle."
SPEAKER_00: "In other words, they surrounded the viewer."
SPEAKER_00: "Incidentally, the word panorama, which is part of our everyday language now, was actually invented by a friend of Robert Barker, who was the first successful painter of this new art form."
SPEAKER_00: "Marker painted the first official panorama in 1787."
SPEAKER_00: "His accomplishment was remarkable for two reasons."
SPEAKER_00: "First, he figured out how to paint a circular painting so that it looked realistic."
SPEAKER_00: "Without going into all the details, Barker found a solution for the complicated perspective problems that this kind of painting presented."
SPEAKER_00: "The other key thing is that Barker recognized the need for a special viewing space to display this new kind of a painting."
SPEAKER_00: "In fact, a new kind of building."
SPEAKER_00: "Other people at that time were coming up with similar ideas, but Barker was the one who had the foresight to get a patent for it, which meant he had the right to the invention."
SPEAKER_00: "He described it as an apparatus for displaying views of nature at large."
SPEAKER_00: "The patent description said that the building was to be circular and it was only to allow light in from above."
SPEAKER_00: "It even specified the height of the platforms people would stand on and how far away they should be from the painting."
SPEAKER_00: "When Barker opened his new panorama building in London in 1794, it was a big hit."
SPEAKER_00: "But he didn't have a monopoly on his invention for long, as his license expired in 1801."
SPEAKER_00: "And in fact, after 1800, panoramas multiplied rapidly."
SPEAKER_00: "Practically every major city in Europe and North America had special buildings for displaying them."
SPEAKER_00: "Most of the buildings and paintings have disappeared, so we don't talk about panoramas much anymore."
SPEAKER_00: "But they were a very popular form of entertainment in their day, which doesn't mean that they weren't good art."
SPEAKER_00: "Many panoramas were painted under the supervision of first-rate artists who were aiming for a faithful reproduction of an actual scene."
SPEAKER_00: "Panoramic artists developed a number of techniques to intensify the illusion of reality for viewers, like special lighting effects."
SPEAKER_00: "And they would hide the edges of the painting with curtains, so there was no appearance of a frame or border at the top or bottom."
SPEAKER_00: "Sometimes they placed plants and trees in the space between the viewer and the painting."
SPEAKER_00: "In that respect, you see, panoramas were very much part of this new movement of realism."
SPEAKER_00: "Of course, paintings of large vistas had been done before."
SPEAKER_00: "It was a common subject for Renaissance painters."
SPEAKER_00: "But panorama painters transformed them by offering a full 360-degree view."
SPEAKER_00: "and the choice of perspective using a bird's eye view and an aerial perspective was new."
SPEAKER_00: "It's probably not a coincidence that hot air balloons made their debut around that time, or that the first high-rise buildings were going up."
SPEAKER_00: "As one writer put it, people were developing a panoramic consciousness."
SPEAKER_00: "Interestingly, a popular subject for panoramas was detailed views of the cities in which they were exhibited."
SPEAKER_00: "It can seem strange today that people were fascinated by detailed recreations of places they already knew, but the fact that the scenes were familiar was apparently part of the fascination."
SPEAKER_00: "One panorama that millions went to see was called A View of Paris from the Roof of the Tuileries."
SPEAKER_00: "Now, anyone willing to climb to the top of a tower in Paris could see exactly the same view."
SPEAKER_00: "So viewers were checking to see if the artists got the details right."
SPEAKER_00: "And of course, finding that, yes, every last detail was right, they were astonished and impressed."
SPEAKER_00: "Ironically, the word panorama has lasted longer than most examples of the art form itself."
SPEAKER_00: "The owners of the buildings where panoramas were displayed charged admission, and they could make a lot of money, but only if audiences kept coming back."
SPEAKER_00: "So they were forced to change the paintings every year or two, which meant that a lot of panoramas were painted over or they were cut up and sold so that new ones could go up in their place."
SPEAKER_00: "So when their popularity waned in the second half of the 19th century, when illustrated newspapers and photography began to satisfy people's desire for realistic images, there weren't a lot of panoramas left to preserve."

SPEAKER_00: "Creep is caused by the same things as other mass movements."
SPEAKER_00: "Gravity is ultimately responsible, and water affects movement."
SPEAKER_00: "But creep happens slowly."
SPEAKER_00: "That is, the soil moves slowly, only a few centimeters to a meter downhill in a year."
SPEAKER_00: "So how does creep happen?"
SPEAKER_00: "Basically, in a freeze-thaw cycle, winter cold, followed by summer heat, and so on."
SPEAKER_00: "Let's take a look at the diagram in your book."
SPEAKER_00: "When the top layer of soil freezes in winter, it expands, so it pushes up and away from the bedrock layer beneath it."
SPEAKER_00: "Then when the ground warms up in the spring when it thaws, everything contracts."
SPEAKER_00: "But those little loose bits of soil on the top don't go back to their original position."
SPEAKER_00: "As the arrows show, they fall just a bit farther down the slope, thanks to gravity."
SPEAKER_00: "This movement is almost imperceptible, and it's not exciting."
SPEAKER_00: "I mean, you'll never see it on a TV show about the dangerous forces of nature."
SPEAKER_00: "But over the years, you may see curved trees or tilted fences."
SPEAKER_00: "That's evidence of creep."
SPEAKER_00: "Creep can happen in warmer areas too due to wet dry cycles where wet conditions cause the soil to expand and dry conditions cause it to contract."
SPEAKER_00: "But I'm interested in colder regions, especially regions with permafrost, permanently frozen ground."
SPEAKER_00: "The process similar to creep in permafrost areas is called solar flexion."
SPEAKER_00: "Now, in solar flexion, the soil moves uniformly down the slope as one large mass, not as individual particles."
SPEAKER_00: "Why?"
SPEAKER_00: "Well, in summer, the top layer of soil above the permafrost, the active layer as we call it, will melt."
SPEAKER_00: "Now, at this point, this active layer is saturated with water and heavier, allowing gravity to do its thing."
SPEAKER_00: "Below the active layer, the ground is still frozen, which is a perfect slide to move on."
SPEAKER_00: "Now in Spitzbergen, I'm sorry, but I hope you're not tired of hearing about the Arctic in Norway, but it's my area of interest, as you know."
SPEAKER_00: "Anyway, there's a great example there."
SPEAKER_00: "It's on the foot of a slope."
SPEAKER_00: "Look at this picture."
SPEAKER_00: "These are stumps left over from a 1940s era structure."
SPEAKER_00: "Even though the bottom of the stumps are in permanently frozen ground, the tops have been pushed over this far."
SPEAKER_00: "It's a perfect visual example of this slow process."
SPEAKER_00: "In the Spitsbergen area, there are varying rates of solar flexion."
SPEAKER_00: "In some areas, it's slower than in others."
SPEAKER_00: "Now, why would that be?"
SPEAKER_00: "Well, as you know, mass wasting is primarily a function of slope and gravity aided by water, right?"
SPEAKER_00: "Well, we can add one more factor."
SPEAKER_00: "We think that in some areas of Spitsbergen, strong fast winds blow snow off the slopes, meaning the effective precipitation, the snow that actually stays on the ground, is small."
SPEAKER_00: "so we don't have as active an active layer of thawed soil, and therefore you get a slower rate of solar flexion."
SPEAKER_00: "With all this in mind, you know, building on hills isn't necessarily the best idea in my opinion, but we do it partly because slopes can be stabilized in various ways."
SPEAKER_00: "Building on permafrost, though, is a different story."
SPEAKER_00: "You simply shouldn't build on it, especially on a slope."
SPEAKER_00: "I said on it, directly on top."
SPEAKER_00: "You want to use posts to elevate the building if you have to build it all."
SPEAKER_00: "This way, you don't overheat the surface."
SPEAKER_00: "That is, the freezing air can flow under the structure, keeping the permafrost intact."
SPEAKER_00: "Now, if you do heat it up, you're risking that an otherwise normally gradual process speeds up."
SPEAKER_00: "So, instead of sole affluxion, the slow, regular process, you destabilize the ground."
SPEAKER_00: "I mean, then you get slides or flows instead, and all the problems they cause."

SPEAKER_00: "If the parents have to fly all over the place to find the food, they'd need to be able to find the nest again."
SPEAKER_01: "And once you've found food, you'd want to get home quickly, wouldn't you, to feed your young?"
SPEAKER_01: "So you'd want the most direct path home."
SPEAKER_01: "You wouldn't want to retrace whatever winding meandering path you'd taken while you were searching for food."
SPEAKER_01: "Being able to fly home directly is very efficient."
SPEAKER_01: "The offspring get food faster, and their parents are home sooner to look out for them."
SPEAKER_00: "But it isn't just the offspring that benefit."
SPEAKER_00: "The adult birds get back to a safe place sooner as well."

SPEAKER_01: "Now, what about finding your way home after going south for the winter?"
SPEAKER_02: "Well, it'd be nice if you could go back to a place you knew was okay."
SPEAKER_02: "You know, one that's got water or food?"
SPEAKER_02: "Not too many predators."
SPEAKER_02: "Some place where you might even still have a nest."
SPEAKER_02: "Good."

SPEAKER_01: "But are there other reasons why a bird might find itself away from its nest?"
SPEAKER_02: "Birds are vulnerable to really strong winds."
SPEAKER_02: "Storms could blow them pretty far off course."
SPEAKER_01: "But finding your way back to your nest when you've been accidentally displaced is a little different from getting home after your annual migration to a warmer climate."
SPEAKER_01: "So, do you think all those mechanisms we discussed that birds use when migrating apply to homing as well?"
SPEAKER_01: "I think so."
SPEAKER_02: "I mean, we talked about using the sun and the stars as navigational guides, and that would certainly be helpful for homing."
SPEAKER_02: "And so would that ability to sense Earth's magnetic poles, the one in the studies you described about the internal magnetic compass birds have."
SPEAKER_02: "Yeah, it should be the same."
SPEAKER_01: "Okay, so celestial bodies make good navigational aids."
SPEAKER_01: "Anything else?"
SPEAKER_00: "Well, for short distances you could just memorize the area, use landmarks, like when my friend tells me to turn left after I see the post office to get to his house."
SPEAKER_01: "So, when birds are out searching for food, they can memorize landmarks."
SPEAKER_01: "Mountains, rivers, who knows, maybe even the post office."
SPEAKER_01: "Studies of Gannets have shown that this is one way they navigate."
SPEAKER_01: "Gannets are seabirds, so when they wind up in an unfamiliar location over land, they fly in outward spirals until they reach the coast."
SPEAKER_01: "Then they use the coastline to find their way home."
SPEAKER_01: "Of course, when we say that birds memorize their terrain, that may not be accurate."
SPEAKER_01: "It's not always a good idea to use human analogies when trying to understand animal behavior, particularly when it comes to navigation, which we as a species are spectacularly bad at."
SPEAKER_01: "I mean, even with maps, how often do we get lost?"
SPEAKER_01: "But with birds, they're actually over-engineered to be good at navigation."
SPEAKER_01: "Just think about all those tools we've discussed."
SPEAKER_01: "Celestial bodies, magnetic fields, landmarks, they've got a lot of sensory cues at their disposal."
SPEAKER_01: "But how do they use them?"

SPEAKER_01: "Does the answer depend on the particular species?"
SPEAKER_01: "This is a really rich subject for research."
SPEAKER_01: "In fact, let's talk about how you might go about designing some experiments to answer these questions."
SPEAKER_01: "Keeping in mind, as I said, that your personal experiences won't be of much use as you try to come up with hypotheses to test."

prompt_text:
Create a visual aid for the passage:

SPEAKER_00: "So you're reading assignment focused on the technology, telescopes, and how they've changed astronomy."
SPEAKER_00: "And there was a section on the Hooker telescope."
SPEAKER_00: "So why was the Hooker telescope so important?"
SPEAKER_00: "Let's talk about that before we move on to some of the newer technologies."
SPEAKER_00: "Now back in the 1920s, the Hooker telescope was the most powerful telescope in the world."
SPEAKER_00: "And through it, we could see, see for the first time, that the universe extends far, far beyond the Milky Way Galaxy."
SPEAKER_00: "We could see that there are multiple galaxies beyond it."
SPEAKER_00: "And all these galaxies are moving away from one another."
SPEAKER_00: "So, multiple galaxies, moving away from each other, led to the theory..."
SPEAKER_00: "The Big Bang Theory?"
SPEAKER_00: "Exactly."
SPEAKER_00: "And that theory that 15 billion years ago when explosions started the expansion of the universe, well, at the time, that was a pretty revolutionary idea."
SPEAKER_00: "And the Hooker telescope was responsible for that?"
SPEAKER_00: "Yes, because it allowed us to see that far, and being able to see that far led to, well, it led to the Big Bang Theory."
SPEAKER_00: "But let's move on."
SPEAKER_00: "The reading assignment discussed another tool for scanning the universe."
SPEAKER_00: "Claudia, what can you tell me about interferometers?"
SPEAKER_01: "OK, an interferometer is a machine made of two or more conventional telescopes."
SPEAKER_01: "I think the example they gave in the book was made from six, and they're spread out."
SPEAKER_01: "They're not right next to each other."
SPEAKER_01: "And then the light, the starlight that those telescopes gather, travels through pipes to some computers, and then the computers put all the light together to make one image."
SPEAKER_00: "Okay, good."
SPEAKER_00: "Although maybe that explanation is a bit simplistic."
SPEAKER_00: "There may be a thousand or so meters of pipe carrying the starlight from the telescopes to the computers, and then getting all those components to work together to synchronize so the light from all the different telescopes reaches the computers at exactly the same time."
SPEAKER_00: "That's the challenge of working with interferometers."
SPEAKER_00: "What else can you tell me?"
SPEAKER_00: "What's the advantage here, Paul?"
SPEAKER_00: "All right."
SPEAKER_00: "I think the idea isn't that it sees farther, but that the image you see is a lot sharper."
SPEAKER_00: "There's more detail."
SPEAKER_00: "A lot more detail."
SPEAKER_00: "The image we get is something like 50 times finer than what you get with a single mirror conventional telescope."
SPEAKER_00: "So why is that important?"
SPEAKER_00: "Now think back a minute."
SPEAKER_00: "A couple weeks ago, we looked at Caster in the Gemini constellation."
SPEAKER_00: "What do you remember about that?"
SPEAKER_01: "Well, through our telescope here, a conventional telescope, Caster looked like a single star."
SPEAKER_01: "But you told us that it wasn't."
SPEAKER_01: "It's actually six stars orbiting, kind of dancing around each other."
SPEAKER_01: "Oh, I get it."
SPEAKER_00: "And an interferometer would be able to see six separate stars?"
SPEAKER_00: "Yes."
SPEAKER_00: "Remember, single stars like our sun are the exception, not the rule."
SPEAKER_00: "So more often than not, you have two, three, or more stars clustered around each other, like Caster."
SPEAKER_00: "Not only that, but even a star's dimensions."
SPEAKER_00: "A conventional telescope won't tell you."
SPEAKER_00: "You can't determine a star's dimension because you can't tell exactly how far away it is."
SPEAKER_00: "And there are other things as well."
SPEAKER_00: "How do stars behave as the age?"
SPEAKER_00: "Why do they cluster together?"
SPEAKER_00: "What was our sun like in the past?"
SPEAKER_00: "Can we predict giant flares or periods of dimming?"
SPEAKER_00: "And interferometers will certainly help us find planets."
SPEAKER_00: "And of course, the more planets you find, the greater the possibility of finding planets that may support life."
SPEAKER_00: "So, do you think interferometers will have the kind of impact the Hooker telescope had?"
SPEAKER_00: "I doubt it."
SPEAKER_00: "Remember, Big Bang was a really revolutionary theory."
SPEAKER_00: "But then again, if an interferometer is used to locate a planet that supports life, well that's another story altogether."

SPEAKER_00: "Traditionally, astronomers worked out how old geologic features of planets and moons are by the number of marks on the surface."
SPEAKER_00: "The more craters in one place, say on a lava flow, the more asteroids and comets that place is encountered over time, so the older it must be."
SPEAKER_00: "This seems to make sense for relative age."
SPEAKER_00: "That is, a surface feature with fewer craters is younger than one with more craters."
SPEAKER_00: "But absolute age, actual age, is trickier."
SPEAKER_00: "We have to know exactly how old one surface is."
SPEAKER_00: "For example, we do have a very clear idea of the ages of some surfaces of the moon from rocks we brought back."
SPEAKER_00: "And then this information can allow us to extrapolate the age of another surface that has a similar concentration of craters."
SPEAKER_00: "That's the traditional way to calculate it."
SPEAKER_00: "But two developments have brought this traditional way into question."
SPEAKER_00: "For one, a recent study of the craters on one of Jupiter's moons, Europa, suggests that at least 95% of its small craters were formed by secondary impacts."
SPEAKER_00: "Okay, secondary impacts."
SPEAKER_00: "They're the impacts of the chunks of rock or ice that break off as a result of the primary impact."
SPEAKER_00: "The primary impact refers to the impactor itself, asteroid comet hitting the planet or moon."
SPEAKER_00: "And when that happens, pieces of rock or ice break off and go flying, and when those chunks come back down and smash into the planet, those are the secondary impacts."
SPEAKER_00: "So, using the old way, we would have assumed that this surface of Europa is much older than it might actually be."
SPEAKER_00: "And it's conceivable that a very large strike from an impactor might throw up some fairly large chunks, ones that are larger than some of the smaller direct strikes."
SPEAKER_00: "So we can't use size to determine if a crater is the result of a primary impact or a secondary one."
SPEAKER_00: "And of course, impactors come in different sizes, though actually we think there are fewer small ones than there used to be."
SPEAKER_00: "What really tells us more though is the arrangement, the way the craters are clustered together or not."
SPEAKER_00: "For example, on Venus, the craters are distributed randomly."
SPEAKER_00: "They're all over the place, which is what we'd expect."
SPEAKER_00: "This suggests that there hasn't been much geologic activity lately on Venus, lava or whatever."
SPEAKER_00: "But on Europa, the craters are in clusters."
SPEAKER_00: "And since asteroids come from all directions, if the craters are arranged in bunches, it's a signal, especially if they're arranged in long ray patterns from a center point, that there was a single primary impact that threw fragments outward from the impact site."
SPEAKER_00: "Another thing, primary impactors hit a lot harder and usually more directly than secondary ones."
SPEAKER_00: "So primary craters tend to be deeper, more bowl-shaped, and almost always circular, which isn't the case with secondaries."
SPEAKER_00: "Anyway, now let's assume Europa is representative of the inner solar system."
SPEAKER_00: "That would mean there are a lot more secondaries on Mars or on Earth's Moon or other bodies than we had originally thought."
SPEAKER_00: "And here's some more proof."
SPEAKER_00: "We got our hands on some nice photos of one particular crater on Mars, Zunil."
SPEAKER_00: "And it turns out that this one impact caused many more secondary craters than we had thought."
SPEAKER_00: "I mean like 90 million more."
SPEAKER_00: "So if the impact causing each large primary crater, and Zunil isn't even that big, results in this many secondaries, then most of the craters on Mars must be secondary."
SPEAKER_00: "And that makes sense actually, since if all the craters, especially the small ones, if all of them are primary craters, well, there simply wouldn't have been enough small objects out there in space to account for all of those craters."
SPEAKER_00: "And unfortunately, this means most craters probably aren't at all useful for dating surfaces on Mars."
SPEAKER_00: "So for example, some lava flows on Mars, which have been dated at about 5 million years old, very young, because of the relatively few craters there, well, it might only mean that this area was one of the random areas that wasn't hit by a primary impactor."
SPEAKER_00: "It just makes it less clear."
SPEAKER_00: "This lava flow could be 100 million years old instead."
SPEAKER_00: "In this case, we can't predict the age with any accuracy unless we have actual samples from the planets."
SPEAKER_00: "You know, we're getting great information and photos from our space probes all the time, but they also remind us of just how much more we need to learn."

prompt_text:
Create a visual aid for the passage:

SPEAKER_00: "Okay, I'd like to get started, please."
SPEAKER_00: "Today, we're going to look at some very unusual plants and animals that have evolved on some remote ocean islands."
SPEAKER_00: "And of course, when I say that, the first thing that probably comes to mind is..."
SPEAKER_00: "The Galapagos Islands."
SPEAKER_00: "Right."
SPEAKER_00: "The Galapagos Islands."
SPEAKER_00: "Which are located..."
SPEAKER_01: "In the Pacific Ocean off the coast of South America."
SPEAKER_00: "That's right, about 600 miles off the coast."
SPEAKER_00: "And being so isolated from the mainland, the species that developed there were quite different from those that developed on the mainland."
SPEAKER_00: "And of course, after Charles Darwin observed all of this, it got his wheels turning and he eventually came up with his famous theory of natural selection."
SPEAKER_00: "And that's why you've all heard of the Galapagos Islands."
SPEAKER_00: "But we're not going to talk about the Galapagos Islands today."
SPEAKER_00: "No."
SPEAKER_00: "No, way out in the middle of the Indian Ocean, somewhere between Africa and India, there's another little cluster of violence."
SPEAKER_00: "Not nearly as famous, but every bit is fascinating in my opinion."
SPEAKER_00: "A little group of violence called the Seychelles."
SPEAKER_00: "Now how many have heard of them?"
SPEAKER_00: "Okay, a few of you then."
SPEAKER_00: "Well, it just so happens that I spent some time in the Seychelles a few years ago."
SPEAKER_00: "So they're kind of close to my heart."
SPEAKER_00: "And just like in the Galapagos Islands, an amazing variety of unusual species have evolved there."
SPEAKER_00: "Things you won't find anywhere else in the world."
SPEAKER_00: "For example, one of the things I saw there was a tiny frog that's not a whole lot bigger than an ant."
SPEAKER_00: "In fact, it's one of the smallest vertebrates on Earth."
SPEAKER_00: "Okay, here's a little mystery for you."
SPEAKER_00: "You rarely get frogs colonizing remote ocean islands like the Seychelles."
SPEAKER_00: "You have some other animals that an ocean current could carry out there from the mainland, but frogs are too delicate."
SPEAKER_00: "They couldn't withstand the salt and the sun and so on for that length of time."
SPEAKER_00: "So how do you think the ancestors of these little frogs got there?"
SPEAKER_00: "Ken?"
SPEAKER_00: "People brought them?"
SPEAKER_00: "Um, good guess."
SPEAKER_00: "People have been responsible for transporting a lot of species from one place to another."
SPEAKER_01: "Oh, maybe there used to be some kind of land bridge that used to connect the islands to the mainland, you know, like the one that used to connect Asia and North America?"
SPEAKER_00: "That's close, Ken."
SPEAKER_00: "Actually, the theory is, millions and millions of years ago, the Earth had only two huge land masses, two super continents."
SPEAKER_00: "And then, around 200 million years ago, part of one of these landmasses broke away and began to move slowly out into the ocean."
SPEAKER_00: "And as that chunk moved away, fragments of it also broke off and were left behind."
SPEAKER_00: "And that's how the Seychelles formed."
SPEAKER_00: "And, so the theory goes, many of the plants and animals living on these islands were stranded out there in the middle of the ocean and isolated from the mainland."
SPEAKER_00: "These tiny frogs are just one of the species descended from those stranded species."
SPEAKER_00: "Diane?"
SPEAKER_00: "I'm just curious."
SPEAKER_00: "How did they figure out that the Seychelles were once part of one big continent?"
SPEAKER_00: "Okay, that's a fair question."
SPEAKER_00: "Well, there are two ways that I know of."
SPEAKER_00: "First, you have some species in the Seychelles whose closest relatives are in Asia, and then you have others there that have distant relatives in both Asia and Africa."
SPEAKER_00: "Now, of course, various species of animals and plants can drift or fly from island to island."
SPEAKER_00: "So that in itself doesn't prove anything."
SPEAKER_00: "And then I'm not a geologist, so I don't know all the details."
SPEAKER_00: "But I do know that part of how they came to that conclusion was because the Seychelles are made up of solid granite bedrock."
SPEAKER_00: "Granite is a type of rock that's typical of continents."
SPEAKER_00: "Islands are typically made of volcanic rock."
SPEAKER_00: "So if an island's made of granite, that suggests that it once was part of a continent."
SPEAKER_00: "In fact, the Seychelles are the only islands in the middle of an ocean made of solid granite."
SPEAKER_00: "The Galapagos Islands, the Hawaiian Islands, their products of underwater volcanoes, their compositions very different from that of the Seychelles."
SPEAKER_00: "Uh, okay, where was I?"
SPEAKER_00: "Oh, oh, oh, okay."
SPEAKER_00: "Okay, so then you had a relatively small number of plants and animals isolated for a long time out in the middle of the ocean, maybe with a few newcomers drifting in now and then."
SPEAKER_00: "And so you got some very interesting things evolving."
SPEAKER_00: "And speaking of that, I just happened to have with me a few hundred slides I took when I was there."
SPEAKER_00: "No, no, just kidding."
SPEAKER_00: "But I do have a few slides of some things you've probably never seen before."
SPEAKER_00: "Alright, would someone get the lights please?"

SPEAKER_00: "He didn't use the traditional easel."
SPEAKER_00: "He laid his wall-size canvas flat on the floor so he could move around it and work it from all sides."

SPEAKER_00: "In fact, people used to watch him work in his studio, dripping and pouring paint and other materials onto his canvases."
SPEAKER_00: "This could make you think of Pollock's work as being kind of like wild or chaotic or random."

SPEAKER_00: "Pollock's poor and drip works were quite revolutionary, and at first they shocked the art world."
SPEAKER_00: "Pollock used massive canvases."
SPEAKER_00: "They seemed more like portable murals than anything else."
SPEAKER_00: "A good example of his technique is the painting Autumn Rhythm, which Pollock painted in 1950."
SPEAKER_00: "Autumn Rhythm, at first glance, looks like basically just a whole lot of squiggly lines."
SPEAKER_00: "Rather bizarre, just like a bunch of pointless drips and swirls."
SPEAKER_00: "But if you look closely, you see why it's so admired."
